seed_fact: The project codename for the vector-store migration is 'Project Narwhal', and the engineer who owns it is named Priya.

turns.content: I want to plan out our vector store migration. Quick heads up: the project codename for this migration is 'Project Narwhal', and Priya is the engineer who owns it end to end. Keep that in mind as we go. Let's start by talking about why sqlite-vec failed our benchmark.

Got it -- Project Narwhal, owned by Priya. On sqlite-vec: at 100k vectors it measured p95 105ms on a plain KNN scan, missing the 50ms interactive-fast bar. Even after fixing the hybrid FTS5+vector query plan (using a rowid-prefilter form instead of a naive JOIN), the brute-force KNN scan cost still dominated at that scale. That's why Qdrant was adopted instead, behind the VectorStore interface so the swap is contained.
Right. Now walk me through the tradeoffs of Qdrant vs a hosted vector DB, in detail, as if explaining to a new engineer joining the project.

Self-hosted Qdrant keeps everything on the same box as the rest of the stack -- no network hop to an external vendor, no data leaving the machine, and no per-query billing. The tradeoff is you own the ops burden: backups, upgrades, disk sizing, and monitoring are all your responsibility instead of a vendor's. A hosted vector DB (Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud, etc.) removes that ops burden but introduces network latency per query, a recurring cost that scales with vector count and query volume, and a dependency on an external service's uptime. Given this is a personal, single-box AI platform with no multi-tenant SLA to meet, self-hosted Qdrant is the right call -- the ops burden is manageable at this scale, and keeping data local matters more than offloading maintenance.
Good. Now switch topics -- tell me about the classifier's thinking-mode bug and how it was fixed.

The classifier (Qwen3-1.7B-Q8_0) initially burned its entire 32-token completion budget on internal <think> reasoning and never produced an answer, failing all 5 test calls. The fix was appending a literal '/no_think' line to the end of the user prompt, which suppressed thinking mode entirely -- no chat-template change was needed. After the fix it answered correctly in 0.11-0.14s per call, 5/5 correct. Notably this same trick did NOT work for a 4B model in a separate dispatcher eval, so suppression via that suffix appears to depend on model size/training, not be a universal template fix.
Interesting. Now tell me about the coder quant decision -- Q4 vs Q5 vs Q6.

All three quants of Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B passed the >=100 tok/s bar. Q4_K_M hit 211-212 tok/s bench / 136 tok/s real-gen. Q5_K_M hit 196.7 tok/s bench / 129.4 tok/s real-gen, with comfortable VRAM headroom in the 32GB pool. Q6_K's original blob was corrupted (mid-stream bit corruption, not truncation -- content-length matched exactly but internal tensor data didn't), so it initially failed to load; after a full re-download it loaded cleanly and passed on real-server numbers (103-138 tok/s, correct output 3/3), though llama-bench itself crashes on that specific file+config due to an unrelated VRAM-reservation bug in llama-bench, not the model. The final adopted default is Q5_K_M -- it clears the bar with headroom, and Q6 only offers a marginal quality gain for +3GB VRAM.
Got it. One more topic: explain the difference between the reasoner and coder model roles, and why they're kept separate rather than merged.

Reasoner (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-32B and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-thinking) is tuned for deliberate multi-step logic with visible chain-of-thought, while coder (Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B) is tuned specifically for code generation and debugging. On a debug-diagnosis eval at a proper 4096-token budget, the dedicated coder model got 6/6 correct diagnoses with zero hallucinated fixes, while the best reasoner got 5/6 and the other reasoner got 3/6 with two confidently-wrong hallucinated fixes (recommending a numpy alias that's itself removed, and inventing invalid Flask import syntax). That gap -- especially the coder model never producing a confidently-wrong answer on this set -- is why they're kept as separate routed roles instead of collapsing reasoning and coding into one generalist model.
Great, that's all the background I needed for now. Quick check: what's the codename of the vector-store migration project again, and who owns it?
